The '''1997 Wigan Warriors season''' was the 102nd season in the club's [[rugby league]] history and the second season in the [[Super League]]. Coached by [[Eric Hughes (rugby, born 1950)|Eric Hughes]], the Warriors competed in [[Super League II]] and finished in 4th place, but went on to win the Premiership Final at [[Old Trafford]] against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]. The club also competed in the [[1997 Challenge Cup]], and were knocked out in the fourth round by St Helens.

==Background==
The inaugural [[Super League]] season in 1996 was a disappointing year for Wigan. Although they finished the year with a victory in the [[1996 Rugby League Premiership|1996 Premiership]] final, the club ended the league season as runners-up to rivals [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]], the first time since 1989 that they had failed to win the league, and their nine-year unbeaten run in the [[Challenge Cup]] had come to an end. From the start of the 1997 season, the club became known as Wigan Warriors.<ref>{{cite news |title=New name for Wigan |url=https://archive.org/details/NewsUK1996UKEnglish/Oct%2031%201996%2C%20The%20Times%2C%20%2365723%2C%20UK%20%28en%29/page/n43/mode/2up?view=theater |work=The Times |date=31 October 1996 |page=45}}</ref>

The club was struggling with financial problems, and was reportedly over £3 million in debt. [[Dave Whelan]], owner of the town's football club [[Wigan Athletic F.C.|Wigan Athletic]], had offered to buy the rugby club's ground, [[Central Park (Wigan)|Central Park]], which would be redeveloped into an all-seater stadium and shared by both clubs. In January 1997, Wigan Warriors' shareholders voted in favour of accepting the offer,<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hadfield |first1=Dave |title=Rugby league : Wigan vote to stay at home |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-wigan-vote-to-stay-at-home-1282854.html |website=The Independent |access-date=2 June 2024 |date=12 January 1997}}</ref> but two months later the board instead decided to sell the ground to [[Tesco]], who had offered considerably more money than Whelan, and the club intended to share the newly built [[Reebok Stadium]] with [[Bolton Wanderers F.C.|Bolton Wanderers]] until it could build its own new stadium.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hadfield |first1=Dave |title=Rugby League: Wigan exiled by £12m Central Park sale |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-wigan-exiled-by-pounds-12m-central-park-sale-1271381.html |website=The Independent |access-date=2 June 2024 |date=6 March 1997}}</ref> The decision led to the departure of director John Martin, who had backed Whelan's proposal.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hadfield |first1=Dave |title=Resignation adds to Wigan's troubles |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/resignation-adds-to-wigan-s-troubles-1280759.html |website=The Independent |access-date=2 June 2024 |date=26 February 1997}}</ref> The shareholders, unhappy that the club was being moved out of town, demanded the resignation of two of the club's board members, Jack Robinson and Tom Rathbone.<ref>{{cite web |title=Wigan chief under fire |url=https://www.theboltonnews.co.uk/news/6184855.wigan-chief-under-fire/ |website=The Bolton News |access-date=2 June 2024 |page=14 March 1997}}</ref> Following months of continuous protests by shareholders and supporters, Robinson and Rathbone resigned in August, with Arthur Thomas taking over as chairman.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hadfield |first1=Dave |title=Rugby League: Reviled Robinson steps down at Wigan |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-reviled-robinson-steps-down-at-wigan-1246433.html |website=The Independent |access-date=2 June 2024 |date=19 August 1997}}</ref> Thomas resigned in October, and the club was taken over by Mike Nolan, a local businessman and former rugby league player with [[Rochdale Hornets]] and [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]].<ref>{{cite news |title=Rugby League: Takeover saves Wigan from going to the wall |url=https://archive.org/details/Independent1997IrelandEnglish/Oct%2031%201997%2C%20Independent%2C%20%233442%2C%20Ireland%20%28en%29/page/n31/mode/2up?view=theater |work=The Independent |date=31 October 1997 |page=32}}</ref> The plan to move in with Bolton Wanderers was dropped, and the club announced they would share a [[Brick Community Stadium|proposed new stadium at Robin Park]] with Wigan Athletic as of the start of the [[2000 Super League season|2000 season]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hadfield |first1=Dave |title=Rugby League: Wigan plan to ground-share with football neighbours Athletic by year 2000 |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-wigan-plan-to-groundshare-with-football-neighbours-athletic-by-year-2000-1295020.html |website=The Independent |access-date=2 June 2024 |date=19 November 1997}}</ref>

Prior to the start of the league season, ''[[The Independent]]'' wrote that Wigan's squad was "thin on genuine quality", and predicted that the team would finish third.<ref>{{cite web |title=Rugby: Club-by-club guide to the Super League |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-clubbyclub-guide-to-the-super-league-1272658.html |website=The Independent |access-date=2 June 2024 |date=13 March 1997}}</ref>

==Match results==
===Challenge Cup===
Wigan were drawn against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] in the fourth round of the [[Challenge Cup]]. Wigan lost 12–26 against their local rivals, with the result eventually leading to the departure of coach [[Graeme West]] a week later.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hadfield |first1=Dave |title=Rugby League: West resigns at Wigan after refusing new role |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-west-resigns-at-wigan-after-refusing-new-role-1279115.html |website=The Independent |access-date=2 June 2024 |date=17 February 1997}}</ref>
